of Day Lewis's Aeneid is most favorable. Estevez
finds that Mantinband (1964)
is more faithful to repetitions than Copley
(1975) or Mandelbaum (1971). Steiner's review of Fitzgerald's Aeneid is
masterly; and Breslin's reflections on war, imperialism and the like in his
assessment of Fitzgerald's version is engaging. John Quincy Adams's review of
Sotheby's translation is an arresting museum piece.

Hering offers a structural analysis of Eclogue 1 (along with Horace, Serm.
2.1, and Odes 3.30; Propertius 1.7 and 8). Kenney's Presidential address to
the Classical Association pleads for exactitude and a return to true philology
in classical studies and examines Eclogue 1.1-2 (pp. 11-12). Anderson asks for
observations on serpents and strawberries, or some other lurking place for
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:11:51 UTC
47
vipers. Freyer regards the riddles of the Third Bucolic as original and appropriate. Springer treats the riddling and playful side of Vergil. Beaujeu favors
an anonymous child in the Messianic Eclogue which signals a new generation
deriving from the Roman aristocracy, a segment of society favoured later by
Octavian-Augustus. Northrup favours another allegorical interpretation, and
Thummer finds more humor than sobriety in the same verses. The Fifth
Bucolic yields Lucretian echoes to Mizera's study. Perret dwells on Daphnis as
a paradigmatic figure, representative of rural civilization, a non-allegorical
pastoral hero, who guarantees peace and forecasts the Golden Age. Deschamps equates the fauns in Eclogue 6 with divinely inspired poets. Camilloni
treats Epicurean principles, ataraxy and unobtrusive living, in the Seventh
Bucolic. Nussbaum treats elision and hiatus as an emotional factor in Eclogue
8.37-41. Viljamaa regards Gallus primarily as an imaginative poet rather than
as soldier or shepherd at Eclogue 10.44-49.

Riccardo Scarcia, "Pauca relicti / iugera ruris" (nota a Georg. 4.125
sqq. e Hor. Carm. 2.15),' Atena e Roma 38 (1983) 63-7.
Eleanor Winsor Leach argues that fortunatus (= M. Terentius Varro) and
felix (Geo. 2.490, 493) both denote Vergil's relationship with literary predecessors who were concerned with the voluptas attendant on a perception of
order in the environment. Geymonat detects reverberations of the Calabrian
ranges not only at Georgics 3.219, but elsewhere in Vergil's opera as well;
there are even recollections in the epic. GrassPs article on the Norie plague is
perceptive and helpful. Bettini's account of Aristaeus' epyllion is a competent
review. Brown vindicates the astronomical accuracy of Vergil and emends
Piscis (Geo. 4.234)
to Pestis. Campbell argues that the Aristaeus scenario
reflects the pattern of an initiation rite, similar to Isiac initiation described by
Apuleius. The myth of Orpheus is regarded as the climax of Aristaeus's initiation, the pinnacle of enlightenment. Crabbe shows that the Aristaeus episode
(or epyllion) subconsciously at the very least influenced Vergil during the
composition of Aeneid 5 and 6. The Palinurus story draws freely on the fates
of both Orpheus and Eurydice, and the preparation for Aeneas' catabasis
develops details from Aristaeus' visit to his mother, Cyrene, and from the
ultimate
sacrifice
in
Geòrgie 4.
Scarcia's
comparative study
of Geòrgie
4.127-8 and Horace, Odes 2.15 offers interesting sidelights on poetic usages.

Edgeworth deals with the six appearances of the purple flower image in the epic; five are connected with the death of a major character or sympathetic figure; the final instance (Aeneid 12.319) features a miraculous restoration. Vergil seems to provide an aetiology for existing ritual practices (Violaria, Rosalia/Rosaria, and Parentalia) which involved casting flowers on graves. Funke's brief address challenges stale conceptions and pleads for bolder, more original criticism of the ideology and composition of the epic. Gillis's study of Eros and Death is a bracing interpretation of the epic along Freudian and Jungian lines, with some address also to Hindu sources. The undertones, ambiguities and latent excitement of Vergil's poetry are a constant concern in Gillis's reflections on the marriage at Carthage, Pallas and Aeneas, Aeneas and Turnus, and Eros and Politics. But the arguments and interpretations will repel more conservative tastes: e.g., 'With the pax Romana, perhaps the human hemorrhage at Rome could finally be staunched; and Roman semen be put to the more productive use for which it was ordained' (p. 144). Hardie finds that gigantomachy themes are present at important points in the epic: the storm (Aeneid l);the Gauls on the Capitol, and Actium, on Aeneas' Shield (Aeneid 8). Mills applies Eliade 's notion of sacred space as an interpretive tool for understanding the epic. According to Mills, the concept is central to the poem's thematic movement and meaning. Monti's study of the Dido Episode wins praise from Putnam for its originality and fresh departures. Nisbet likens the epic to a great novel of war and politics. The imperator finally wins his spolia opima from the defeated Turnus, but his supreme command has eluded him. R. E. Woodrow. Virgil's Aeneid XI: A Commentary on the Camilla
Episode. (Diss., Oxford, 1978. unpublished).
Arrigoni's study offers a rich and tidy array of literary and archaeological
evidence regarding Camilla. Religion (the Diana Cult at Aricia) and poetry are
richly intermingled in a stimulating study of the Amazon and Priestess nature
of the Volscian commander. Arrigone's volume is required reading. Belfiore
studies seven occasions when Aeneas embraces, or longs to embrace, members
of his family. A comparison of the passages reveals one way in which Vergil
portrays emotional change in Aeneas. Della Corte and Feeney are both concerned with Juno, as personality and divine agent, and with her antipathy to
Aeneas and the Roman cause, and not only in Vergil. Feeney argues that
Juno's unmanageable and disquieting nature resists governance; the forces
represented by the goddess are still in tension at the close of the epic: the
good and evil principles that hold the universe in shifting balance find their
embodiment in Juno's power. Moles uses Aristotle's definition of hamartia as
a blueprint for gauging Dido's moral responsibility for her downfall. Aeneid
4.165-72 signals the culpa (or hamartia) which finally destroys the Carthaginian Queen. Her sexual misdemeanour, her submission to Aeneas out of
wedlock, was determining. Aeneid 4.169-72 and 393 are, in Moles's view,
moral signposts. Muecke defines Vergilian irony and by using Aristotle's analysis of the elements of tragic plot, shows that this dramatic irony is basic to
the structure of Vergil's 'tragedy.' Foreshadowing, in Aeneid 1, creates the
sense of dramatic irony which infuses the tragic story. Németh takes a synoptic view of Catullus' Ariadne, Vergil's Dido, and Ovid's Ariadne. West argues
that Dido's one-time faithful love turned to guilty forgetfulness of Sychaeus
is heightened by parallels and contrast with Andromache's fidelity to the
dead Hector. Dido's infirmity is also the infirmity of Carthage. Dyck's treatment of Sychaeus explores the manifold uses Vergil finds for the deceased
husband: to rouse Dido to leave Carthage without taint of treason; to provide
a hindrance to an involvement with Aeneas; to facilitate Dido's transition
from life to death; and to enhance, in death, her heroic stature.

Religion (the Diana Cult at Aricia) and poetry are richly intermingled in a stimulating study of the Amazon and Priestess nature of the Volscian commander. Arrigone's volume is required reading. Belfiore studies seven occasions when Aeneas embraces, or longs to embrace, members of his family. A comparison of the passages reveals one way in which Vergil portrays emotional change in Aeneas. Della Corte and Feeney are both concerned with Juno, as personality and divine agent, and with her antipathy to Aeneas and the Roman cause, and not only in Vergil. Feeney argues that Juno's unmanageable and disquieting nature resists governance; the forces represented by the goddess are still in tension at the close of the epic: the good and evil principles that hold the universe in shifting balance find their embodiment in Juno's power. Moles uses Aristotle's definition of hamartia as a blueprint for gauging Dido's moral responsibility for her downfall. Aeneid 4.165-72 signals the culpa (or hamartia) which finally destroys the Carthaginian Queen. Her sexual misdemeanour, her submission to Aeneas out of wedlock, was determining. Aeneid 4.169-72 and 393 are, in Moles's view, moral signposts. Muecke defines Vergilian irony and by using Aristotle's analysis of the elements of tragic plot, shows that this dramatic irony is basic to the structure of Vergil's 'tragedy.' Foreshadowing, in Aeneid 1, creates the sense of dramatic irony which infuses the tragic story. Németh takes a synoptic view of Catullus' Ariadne, Vergil's Dido, and Ovid's Ariadne. West argues that Dido's one-time faithful love turned to guilty forgetfulness of Sychaeus is heightened by parallels and contrast with Andromache's fidelity to the dead Hector. offers useful insights into Vergil's philosophical inclinations and tenets. Epicureanism is the hallmark of both the
Bucolics and the Georgics for both Grilli and Grimal. Nielson argues that
Aeneas must, according to religious convention, keep his obligations to the
dead. In the case of Mago and Turnus, where the claims of the living are
involved, Aeneas' pietas has to override sympathy. Novara argues that there is
a Socratic tradition implicit in the expression - felix qui potuit rerum cognosces causas (Georgics 2.490) implying a doctrine of happiness based on the
knowledge of causes. Webb detects a consistent scheme in the recurrence of
direct contacts involving the hero and divinities during the epic. There is an
evolving process of response on the hero's part and a system underlies the
supernatural interventions which is almost programmatic in character.

D'Anna studies early responses to Vergil's output and argues that neither
Horace nor Propertius was a fervent admirer of the early Vergil. Fairweather
regards the Qasr Ibrim papyrus, evidently reflecting Gallan verse, as a fragment of an amoebean song-contest. Signs separating the quatrains indicated
where the text of the contest-poem called for a change of singer. The precedent for elegiacs in a singing match exists in (Theocr.) Idyll 8.33-60. Gagé
examines the literary taste and writing of Augustus; the article offers a mine
of useful references. Hardie stresses Horace's indebtedness to Vergil, Georg.
3.1 Off.in the celebrated Odes, 3.30; Horace's linking of poet as triumpbator
with an architectural metaphor, his hope for public recognition, the references to Greek models, to his home town and native river, and the new
imperial vision are common factors. Heyworth suggests that the Gallus fragment does not derive from a continuous poem. The first verse belongs properly to another work; both are components of an anthology of verse, maybe
not exclusively Gallan. Huzar's survey of Antony's stabs at literature contains
a rich collection of references and sheds interesting side-lights on Vergil's
poetry. Philipps' rethinking of Augustan poetry, content, design, and propaganda, deserves wide reading and continuing reflection by Vergilian scholars.
Quinn's reminder that publication for the Augustans was more recitatio and
acroasis than written texts is useful. His treatment of art and society (166-7),
the collegium poetarum,and other matters is interesting and enlightening and
the article is a useful companion to his Martin Classical Lecture (1975), and
to his Texts and Contexts: The Roman Writers and their Audience (Toronto
1979).

unusual position, before the second-foot diaeresis at Aeneid 4.372. Lesueur's

novit agrestis (Geo. 2.493) traces their recurrence and application in patristic
writers.

Jürgen von Stackelberg, 'Vergil, Lalli, Scarron. Ein Auschnitt aus der
Geschichte der Parodie, 'Arcadia 17 (1982) 225-44.
Cottrill's list of art works and monuments in southern England which were
inspired by Vergil's Aeneid is a vade mecum for travellers. Dane finds formal
and thematic order in Chaucer's House of Fame by seeing the tripartite poem
in terms of the medieval rhetorical concept of the rota Virgili by which, as
Geoffrey of Vinsauf remarked, there are found three styles exemplified in Vergil's major works: HnBucolicis humili, in Georgicis mediocri, in Eneyde grandiloqui.
' The House of Fame Part I, has an epic subject and aspirations; II is in
the
middle
or didactic range; and III resembles the lyric pastoral
entertainments of the Eclogues. Doederlein argues that Dryden's interest in literary
pictorialism governed changes to the original in his translation of the Aeneid ;
he praised the painterly aspect of Vergil's poetry in a preface. Edgeworth
adduces Aen. 7.456f. {atro lumine), and the Zohar as inspiration for Milton's
'darkness visible.' Fantazzi's tribute examines Broch's rhapsodic, prolix account of Vergil's final hours.
Hindman's collection contains articles by Eleanor Winsor Leach on 'Illustration as Interpretation in Brant's and Dryden's
Editions of Vergil' (1502, and 1697), and Ruth Mortimer's 'Vergil in the
Rosenwald Collections,' a medley of texts and various illustrated editions.
Johnson examines Eclogue IV and Wordsworth's immortality Ode' for thematic and artistic affinities. The design, Johnson argues, is based on the
geometrical ratio
of the Golden Section, which controls the location
of
symbolic words and sounds. Return or recollection and continuity are central
to the themes and designs of both poems. Kemp deals with illustrated versions of Vergil's works. Levine examines Dryden's All for Love as a reworking
of the action of Aeneid 4. Dryden resolves the problem of abandonment not
by Vergilian but by Senecan principles. Lind mentions Frost's special interest
in Catullus, Vergil, Horace, and particularly Lucretius, and considers the relationship of North of Boston to the genre of the eclogue. Rudat argues that
Milton's opening of Paradise Lost with allusions to Vergil's opening of the
Aeneid suggests parallels between Satan and Juno as the evil forces of the
respective epics. Milton uses the Vergilian questions of the openings as rhetorical devices that create suspense and enhance various aspects of the conflict
between good and evil in both poems. Stehlikova, and Murray extol the
virtues and unique qualities of Hermann Broch's Death of Virgil (1945). Von
Stackelberg studies Scarron 's fanciful, complex, critical parody of Vergil. His
Virgile travesti abides as a paragon in the history of epic parody.
McMaster University
31 July, 1984
